tag: 20 min · weekly audit + reset · do this every sunday
title: The Sunday Reset
intro: Without a weekly reset, drift compounds. You wake up in week six of a pattern you never agreed to. This is the audit that keeps the fire lit and the direction honest — once a week, same time, no skipping.

quote.text: The unexamined week drifts. The examined one gets chosen.

deliverable: Three written outputs: what worked, what drifted, one specific reset for the week ahead. Kept short. Kept weekly.

exercises.title: What Worked
prompt: Name three things from the past week that genuinely worked — not 'I was productive,' but specific moments. A conversation that landed. A decision you made fast that turned out right. A morning routine that actually launched the day. Proof, not praise.

guidance.title: This is evidence-gathering, not a gratitude journal
guidance.body: You're building a case file for what your best week looks like. Over months, the pattern becomes legible — the conditions under which you do your best work. That's the payoff of doing this every week.

exercises.title: What Drifted
exercises.sub: 10 min · name the drift, not the guilt
prompt: Where did you drift this week? Not 'I failed' — 'I agreed on Monday to do X, and by Wednesday I was doing Y.' Drifts are decisions you didn't make consciously. Name them clearly.
For each drift: one line on what caused it. Exhaustion? Distraction? A conversation that moved the goalposts? A mood you let drive a decision? The cause matters more than the drift.

guidance.title: Drift is neutral information
guidance.body: This isn't a self-flagellation exercise. Drift is data. A week without drift means you're playing it too safe. A week with too much drift means your system isn't holding. The ratio is what you want to learn, not the verdict on each item.

guidance.title: Look for the same drift twice
guidance.body: If the same drift shows up this week and last, the reset needs to address the cause, not the symptom. 'I kept skipping the morning write' happening three weeks in a row means the cause is structural, not willpower.

exercises.watch_out: Making the list too long. Three drifts max. If you have more, something bigger is happening and the exercise needs to become a longer therapy session.

exercises.title: One Reset
exercises.sub: 5 min · one specific change for the week ahead
prompt: Name ONE reset for the coming week. Not five. Not a system overhaul. One specific change that directly addresses the biggest drift from 1.2. Write it as a trigger-action: 'When X happens, I will do Y.' Not a vague intention.

guidance.title: Trigger-action format
guidance.body: Bad: 'I'll be more disciplined about mornings.' Better: 'When my alarm goes off, I sit up immediately and put my feet on the floor before checking my phone.' The trigger is what your future self can actually notice. The action is what they can actually do.

guidance.title: One is the whole point
guidance.body: You can't reset five things at once. Pick the one that would compound the most if it held. The others can wait for next week. Real change is sequential, not parallel.

exercises.tip: Do this exercise at the same time every week. Sunday evening is traditional for a reason — the week ahead is visible but not yet consequential. Set a recurring calendar block.
